sublevels.title: C ARRAYS & POINTERS INTRO
sublevels.subtitle: Memory, arrays, and the address-of operator

teaching.title: C Arrays & Memory
teaching.analogy: A C array is a raw block of memory — like a strip of paper with equally-spaced numbered boxes. No bounds checking, no automatic resizing, no safety net. Power and danger in equal measure.
teaching.content: C arrays: fixed size, zero-indexed, no bounds checking. Declared as type name[size]. Stack-allocated arrays are automatically freed. Array name IS a pointer to the first element. sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) gives element count.

examples.title: C Arrays
examples.explanation: Raw memory — no safety, maximum speed.

examples.title: Array Name = Pointer
examples.explanation: Array name decays to pointer to first element.

teaching.funFact: C's array-pointer duality is one of the most powerful (and dangerous) features. arr[i] is literally defined as *(arr + i). So 5[arr] is valid C and equals arr[5]! This is why C is called 'portable assembly.' 🔥

teaching.title: Pointers — The Basics
teaching.analogy: A pointer is a sticky note with an address written on it. The address tells you WHERE the actual data lives in memory. & gets the address. * follows the address to get the data (dereference).
teaching.content: Pointer: variable storing a memory address. & (address-of) gets a variable's address. * (dereference) follows a pointer to its value. int *p = &x; declares a pointer. *p = 5; changes the value at that address.

examples.title: Basic Pointers
examples.code:
int x = 42;
int *p = &x;  // p holds address of x

printf("%d\n", x);   // 42 (value)
printf("%p\n", &x);  // 0x... (address of x)
printf("%p\n", p);   // same address as &x
printf("%d\n", *p);  // 42 (dereference: value AT address)

*p = 100;  // change x through pointer
printf("%d\n", x);  // 100 (x is changed!)

teaching.funFact: Pointers are the reason C is used in OS kernels, embedded systems, and device drivers. They give you DIRECT hardware access — you can map a pointer to any memory address, including hardware registers! 💻

mcq.explanation: &arr[0]: The & operator gives the MEMORY ADDRESS of arr[0]. This is the same as the array name itself. C arrays decay to pointers to their first element.
Dereference: *p follows the pointer p to whatever it points at. p = &x (address of x). *p = value at that address = 7.

mcq.q: int arr[5]; sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) when int=4 bytes:

mcq.explanation: No Safety: C arrays have ZERO bounds checking. Accessing arr[100] on a 5-element array is undefined behavior — might crash, corrupt memory, or silently return garbage.

options: A pointer to int — stores an address
mcq.explanation: Pointer: 'int *p' declares p as a pointer to int — it stores a memory address of an int variable. The * is part of the type, not a dereference here.

coding.title: Dereference operator
coding.objective: int x=5; int *p=&x; What does *p return?
coding.hint: *p dereferences pointer p — gives value at address (x's value = 5).

coding.title: sizeof element count
coding.hint: 6 elements of int type. sizeof gives 6.

options: arr[3] is out of bounds — arr has indices 0,1,2 only. Undefined behavior!
debugging.answer: arr[3] is out of bounds — arr has indices 0,1,2 only. Undefined behavior!
debugging.hint: 3-element array → valid indices 0,1,2. arr[3] reads past the end — undefined behavior.

options: p should be int* (pointer type), not int
debugging.answer: p should be int* (pointer type), not int
debugging.hint: &x is a memory address. It must be stored in a pointer: int *p = &x; not int p = &x;

prediction.explanation: *p = 99 sets a's value to 99 via pointer. b is untouched. Output: 99 10.

teaching.title: C Strings — char Arrays
teaching.analogy: C strings are arrays of characters ending with a secret null terminator '\0'. Like a secret stop sign at the end of a parade — C functions march through characters until they hit \0. Without it, functions keep reading memory indefinitely!
teaching.content: C strings are char[] ending with '\0' (null terminator). Declared as char name[] = 'hello'; (auto-null) or char name[6] = {'h','e','l','l','o','\0'}. Use <string.h> for string functions: strlen, strcpy, strcat, strcmp.

examples.title: C String Basics
string.h Functions

teaching.funFact: The null terminator '\0' has caused countless security vulnerabilities. strcpy doesn't check buffer size — it copies until '\0'. A string longer than the buffer causes a buffer overflow, potentially allowing code execution! 🔐

mcq.explanation: \0: Every C string MUST end with '\0' (null character, ASCII 0). C string functions rely on it to know where the string ends. Missing it causes buffer overruns.
strlen: Returns number of characters BEFORE the null terminator. 'hello' = 5 chars + 1 null. strlen = 5 (null not counted). sizeof(s) = 6 (includes null).

mcq.q: To compare C strings for equality, use:
mcq.explanation: strcmp: s1==s2 compares POINTER addresses (almost always false for separate arrays). strcmp(s1,s2) compares CONTENT — returns 0 if equal.

options: Appends src to end of dest
mcq.explanation: strcat (string concatenate): Appends src to the END of dest. dest must have enough space! DANGER: no bounds checking — overflows if dest is too small.

coding.title: strcmp equal
coding.objective: strcmp('hello','hello') returns what for equal strings?
coding.hint: strcmp returns 0 when strings are identical.

options: a==b compares pointers (addresses), not content. Use: if(strcmp(a,b)==0)
debugging.answer: a==b compares pointers (addresses), not content. Use: if(strcmp(a,b)==0)
debugging.hint: In C, == on arrays compares memory addresses. Always use strcmp() for string content comparison.

teaching.title: Structs — C's Data Grouping
teaching.analogy: A struct is like a form with multiple fields — a player profile has a name field, age field, and score field all bundled together. No methods, no access control — just pure data grouping. C's alternative to OOP classes.
teaching.content: struct groups related variables of different types. Declare with struct keyword. Access fields with . (dot) for direct struct, -> (arrow) for pointer to struct. typedef creates a shorthand name.

examples.title: Defining & Using Structs
typedef & Pointer to Struct
examples.explanation: typedef removes 'struct' keyword. -> accesses via pointer.

teaching.funFact: C structs inspired Java classes! Classes are essentially structs with methods attached. The struct became the building block of OOP — C++ literally added method syntax to C structs to create objects! 🏗️

mcq.q: How do you access 'score' field of struct Player p?
mcq.explanation: Dot operator: Use . to access struct fields on a direct struct variable. Use -> to access through a pointer: ptr->score.

mcq.q: What does -> operator do with structs?
options: Accesses field through a pointer to struct
mcq.explanation: Arrow ->: ptr->field is shorthand for (*ptr).field. When you have a pointer to struct, use -> to access fields. Direct struct uses dot (.).

mcq.q: What does typedef struct { ... } Enemy; allow?
options: Using 'Enemy' instead of 'struct Enemy' as the type
mcq.explanation: typedef: Creates an alias. Without typedef, you'd write 'struct Enemy e;'. With typedef, just 'Enemy e;' — cleaner and looks more like modern types.

options: No — structs are data-only in C; methods are a C++ feature
mcq.explanation: Data Only: C structs cannot have functions/methods. They're pure data containers. C++ extended structs to allow methods — which eventually became classes.

coding.title: Dot access
coding.objective: struct Point{int x,y;}; Point p={3,7}; p.x = ?
coding.hint: p.x accesses the x field of point p = 3.

coding.title: Arrow operator

options: Can't assign string literals to char arrays. Use strcpy(p.name, 'Neo');
debugging.answer: Can't assign string literals to char arrays. Use strcpy(p.name, 'Neo');
debugging.hint: char arrays can't use = for string assignment (after declaration). Use strcpy() from <string.h>.

prediction.explanation: p->x=10 changes a.x to 10 through pointer. a.y stays 4.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C MEMORY MASTER
teaching.analogy: The Memory Master controls every byte of your program's stack and heap. It knows about every null terminator, every pointer dereference, every buffer overflow. Prove your C data handling is bulletproof!
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 4 C: array access/sizeof formula, pointer syntax &/*->, C strings/null terminator, strlen vs sizeof, strcmp for comparison, strcat/strcpy, struct dot/arrow access, typedef.

examples.title: Full C Data Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ int arr[n] — no bounds check!
✅ sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) — length
✅ int *p = &x; *p = value;
✅ arr name decays to pointer!
✅ char str[] — null terminated \0
✅ strlen (no null) vs sizeof (with null)
✅ strcmp for string compare (not ==!)
✅ struct + . (direct) or -> (pointer)

teaching.funFact: C developers who master pointers and memory layout can write code that runs on hardware from microcontrollers (32 bytes RAM!) to supercomputers. The same C concepts work at every scale — that's why C is still #1 in systems programming! 🏆

mcq.explanation: *p=42 dereferences p and assigns 42 to whatever p points to — which is x. So x becomes 42.
strlen('cat')=3 (chars before \0). sizeof('cat' array)=4 (includes \0). Remember: strlen excludes null, sizeof includes it.

mcq.q: To compare char arrays in C, use:
mcq.explanation: strcmp: == compares pointers. strcmp(a,b)==0 compares content. Always use strcmp for C string equality!
Arrow operator: Through pointer, (*p).y or (equivalently) p->y. Both are valid — -> is the shorthand.

mcq.q: C struct vs Java class — key difference?
options: C struct is data-only; Java class has methods, access control, inheritance
mcq.explanation: Data Only: C struct groups data. Java class groups data + methods + constructors + access modifiers. C++ bridged this by adding methods to structs.

options: Buffer overflow! 'Alexander' needs 10 bytes but name is only 5. Increase char name[10] or use strncpy.
debugging.answer: Buffer overflow! 'Alexander' needs 10 bytes but name is only 5. Increase char name[10] or use strncpy.
debugging.hint: strcpy has no bounds checking. Copying 9 chars into 5-byte buffer overflows into adjacent memory — dangerous!

prediction.explanation: p=arr points to first element (2). p+3 points to 4th element (index 3). *(p+3) = arr[3] = 8.
